summary: The post argues that while AI tools make it feel like there has never been a better time to start a startup, the pace of improvement creates a deflationary effect: waiting even a few months can dramatically lower the cost and raise the quality of what you can build. That dynamic turns today’s frontier into tomorrow’s tech debt, making early-built products feel obsolete faster and raising the question of whether starting now is actually optimal.
money_quotes: If all you think about is the tools that are available to you, then today is always a better time to start a company than yesterday, and today will always be worse than tomorrow.
But what if those costs are falling quickly?
Almost immediately, the new one is better. It’s more durable; it’s faster; it’s better designed.
There is no fixed definition of tech debt—it is simply code that the current engineers would prefer to be written differently.
After all, you can rename your company to keep up with the latest fads, but it’s not so easy to rebuild it.
key_insight: Rapidly improving AI tools create a deflationary dynamic in software creation, making each new wave of tooling render recent work obsolete and complicating the idea that now is always the best time to start.
